food_subgroup: Beef dishes — stir-fried / sizzling plate preparations

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for hot plate spring onion beef. FOODON_00002764 (beef dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for sizzling-plate spring onion beef preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this specific dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of hot plate spring onion beef is approximately 250 g, comprising sliced beef, spring onions, and sauce served sizzling on a cast-iron hot plate.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, stir-fried with vegetables and soy sauce — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 27066) adapted with spring onion beef stir-fry composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for hot plate spring onion beef exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy beef stir-fry with vegetables (NDB 27066) and cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods beef (sliced, cooked) and spring onion composition data. Sodium is elevated to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning typical of this dish. Values represent a reasonable estimate for a restaurant-prepared version.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot plate spring onion beef. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous stir-fried beef with vegetables and soy-based sauce, which is a low-GI mixed dish dominated by protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate contribution. Carbohydrate content per 100g is approximately 5.2g, primarily from cornstarch and spring onion. GL per serving (250g) = (30 × 13.0g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.9. Reference: Atkinson FS et al. (2021), International tables of GI and GL values, AJCN.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 520 mg per 100g, equating to ~1300 mg per standard 250g serving), which represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot plate spring onion beef has a low estimated GI (~30) and a low glycemic load per serving (~3.9), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (~2.6g per 100g) and high sodium (~520 mg per 100g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and request reduced-sauce preparations to lower sodium and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Plate Spring Onion Beef is a popular Filipino-influenced sizzling dish served at casual dining restaurants and Filipino cafes in Singapore. The dish features thinly sliced beef, typically from sirloin or flank cuts, stir-fried at high heat with generous amounts of fresh spring onions (scallions) in a savoury sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and sesame oil. The beef is often tenderised with a cornstarch marinade before cooking. The dish is served on a preheated cast-iron hot plate that continues to sizzle at the table, creating an aromatic and visually dramatic presentation. The flavour profile is savoury, slightly sweet from the oyster sauce, with a mild onion fragrance from the spring onions. The texture contrasts tender, juicy beef slices with the slight crunch of lightly cooked spring onion. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of protein (~14.5g per 100g), iron (~2.1 mg per 100g), zinc (~3.5 mg per 100g), and vitamin B12 (~1.2 mcg per 100g), making it nutritionally dense for a meat-based dish. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content due to soy and oyster sauce seasoning, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is low, making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when not paired with large portions of white rice. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found at Filipino restaurants and cafes such as Lucky Cafe, and is typically priced around SGD 20 per plate.
common_user_queries: What is hot plate spring onion beef?
How many calories in hot plate spring onion beef?
Is sizzling beef good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for hot plate spring onion beef was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy beef stir-fry with vegetables, cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods beef and spring onion data. GI is estimated by analogy from peer-reviewed GI tables for similar stir-fried beef dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically sound and the source data is from an approved primary database.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source database for this food category. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated values from USDA FoodData Central cross-reference. Sodium value (~520 mg per 100g) is elevated above the base USDA stir-fry reference to reflect the soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning typical of Filipino-style hot plate beef preparations. The dish is served at Lucky Cafe (North Canal Road, Singapore) and is listed on field survey data at SGD 20.00. Halal status is marked false as beef sourcing and halal certification for this specific restaurant have not been confirmed. The dish is Filipino in origin but is served in a Singapore context; multilingual names follow the Singaporean dish multilingual rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
